   network utilizing high-frequency (RF) bands,  such as
T
1 millimeter waves and terahertz frequencies,  to address  the
v
escalating demand for system capacity driven by emerging
4
intelligent applications and services (e.g., AR, VR, etc). The
5
9 utilization of high RF bands necessitates the deployment of
1 extremely large antenna arrays (ELAA),  allowing for high
2 data rates and enhanced coverage[1]. However, the increased
.
3 number of  antenna elements  in ELAA,  coupled with  the
0 smaller wavelengths, makes  the  far-field  assumptions  of
5
conventional wireless communication no  longer hold  true
2
: for certain users. Specifically,  for users  located within the
v near-field region (i.e., tens of meters or hundreds of meters),
i
the electromagnetic (EM) wavefront  is spherical rather than
X
r planar[2]. Consequently, the near-field steering vector depends
a not only on the angle but also on the distance. Therefore,
conventional beamforming and beam training methods  that
use a DFT-based far-field codebook to sweep the angular
domain  are  insufficient  for  near-field  users  (NUs).  For
instance, [3] proposed a polar-domain codebook for NUs, that
incorporates both angular and distance  information, which
has been successfully demonstrated  in beam training [4].
Additionally, [5] designed off-grid codewords for multi-user
MIMO beam focusing considering different antenna structures.
This paper was produced by the IEEE Publication Technology Group. They
are in Piscataway, NJ.
Zijun Wang and Rui Zhang are with the Department of Electrical Engineer-
ing, The State University of New York at Buffalo, New York, USA (email:
zwang267@buffalo.edu, rzhang45@buffalo.edu).

m Training with DFT Codebook
attern Analysis
ang, member, IEEE
However,  the aforementioned codebooks exhibit relatively
high operational complexity,  as  the beam training requires
exhaustive  searches across both  the angular  and distance
domains,  leading to an increased codebook size. Developing
a low-complexity beam training procedure is critical for NUs.
Therefore, [4] proposed a two-stage beam training procedure.
In the first stage, a coarse search is performed in the angular
domain using a DFT codebook and a subarray. The second
stage conducts a refined search across both the angular and
distance domains utilizing the near-field codebook[3] and the
full ELAA. [6] showed that reducing the number of antenna
elements decreases  the Rayleigh distance [7], making the
beam patterns of NUs resemble those of FUs. As a result,
the DFT codebook remains applicable for near-field beam
training. Building on this, [8] provided a numerical analysis
of the beam gain pattern and proposed methods to mitigate
interference from far-field DFT codewords on NUs during
beam training. Furthermore,  [9]  introduced a fast  two-stage
beam training method,  employing DFT codewords  in  the
first  stage to obtain the angle using the central portion of
the beam pattern,  followed by refining both  the distance
and angle in the second stage using near-field codebook[3].
Similarly,  [10] demonstrated  that DFT codewords can be
utilized to jointly estimate the angle and distance of NUs by
numerically analyzing the beam width and beam gain power,
yielding  lower  complexity  than other methods. Recently,
deep learning approaches have also been explored to further
improve beam training performance with DFT codebooks
[11].  It uses far-field wide-beam measurements to estimate
and refine the optimal near-field beam by neural networks.
Despite  these  advancements,  existing  studies  on  low-
complexity near-field beam training using the DFT codebook
primarily rely on numerical simulations or sophisticated AI.
In this paper, we firstly derive the closed-form expressions of
the received beam pattern width and gain for NUs using DFT
codebooks.  In particular, we propose a modified Rayleigh
distance based on the beam width, numerically analyze the
variation of beam width with respect  to angle and distance
and the beam pattern gain as a function of distance and angle.
Based on the derivations, we further propose a low-complexity
beam training method followed by simulation analysis.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section II.
presents the system model. Section III analyzes the received
beam pattern, deriving closed-forms for beam pattern width
and beam pattern gain. Based on the theoretical derivation,
.00 © 2025 IEEE

In this article, we consider a base station (BS) with the
uniform linear array (ULA) consisting of N antenna elements,
and user equipment (UE) is equipped with one single antenna.
The BS adopts DFT codebook to perform beam training in the
angular domain.

A. Modified Rayleigh Distance
The  conventional  Rayleigh Distance  is  based  on  the
criterion  that  the maximum phase error  for  each antenna
π
element  does  not  exceed  8 . However,  these boundaries
do not  account  for  communication performance or beam
management losses. Therefore, we define a modified Rayleigh
Distance for beam training based on the normalized beam
(cid:0) H  (cid:1)
pattern Gnorm h (θ, r), a(φ)  to better distinguish NUs and
FUs.
For ULA of N antenna elements, the resolution of far-field
2
beam pattern [1] will be N . We define the modified Rayleigh
distance based on the fact that, using DFT codebook, the beam
ρ  p  2
width B (θ, r) should be larger than a threshold ∆θ = p · N ,
where p is a small constant. Larger p will  lead to smaller
modified Rayleigh Distance and vice versa. Thus, at certain
ρ,p

equation

Based on the definitions, we propose a near-field beam
training scheme by jointly estimating user angle and distance.
B. Estimate Angle by Middle Theta
For a user located at (θ, r), we first perform beam sweeping
using the DFT codebook to obtain the receive signal ampli-
tude:

I = {n | |y(vn)| > ρ2}.  (28)
Next, we partition I into clusters based on proximity. Specif-
′
ically,  two consecutive indices n and n belong to the same
′
cluster if n −n ≤ L,where L is a fixed small number. If L is
too large, peaks due to noise variations may be grouped with
the main-beam peaks, contaminating the cluster and degrading
the angle estimation. Conversely,  if L is too small, adjacent
peaks may be split into separate clusters. In our algorithm, we
choose L = 8.

cluster by finding the strongest received signal within it. The

VI. NUMERICALSTIMULATION (a) user location (0,10m)

derivations and the proposed beam training scheme. We set
N = 512 , carrier frequency f = 100GHz and d = λ =

C. Proposed Beam Training Results
We set  the reference SNR as the SNR of a user at (0, 5)
without  beamforming. With fixed  transmitted power, we
sweep different noise power by setting reference SNR from
4 dB to 30 dB. We set the user locations within the modified
Rayleigh Distance and thus all users are NUs.
Firstly, we test  the accuracy of our methods,  referring to
the Coarse scheme and Refined scheme. We test the influence
of different k using the middle-k-selection scheme on the pro-
posed two approach. In a single-user scenario, the BS utilizes
the beamforming vector v = b(θˆ, rˆ), which is determined
using the estimated angle θˆ and distance rˆ.

1) Joint angle and distance estimation (Joint scheme). This
is proposed in [10]. We set k = 3 for a fair comparison.
2) Fast estimation using DFT code (Fast scheme). This is
proposed in [9]. We also consider k = 3.
3) Exhausted beam training (Exhausted scheme). This is to
use the near-field codebook proposed in [3]. We set β =
1.6, which is the same as in [3].
The results can be found in Figs. 8 and 9.  It can be seen
that our two proposed scheme achieves the lowest NMSE, and
the gap is more significant, especially in high SNR scenario.
The refined scheme also achieves a significant gain in distance
estimation when SNR is high.

Thirdly, we evaluate the impact of DFT codebook sample
rate on different schemes. The sample rate of DFT codebook
denotes the number of uniformly sampled angles. As stated
in [10],  the fast  scheme is sensitive to the sample rate of
the DFT codebook. On the other hand,  the performance of
Joint scheme will  increase using the over-sampling strategy.
Thus we compare the performance of Coarse scheme, Refined
scheme and Joint scheme using different sample rates. We set
the reference SNR is 20dB to avoid the influence of noise. We
test  the NMSE of distance and angle on 100 NUs for Joint
scheme, proposed scheme and refined scheme. The results are
shown in Tables I and II  It can be seen that although all
three methods are sensitive to the sample rate, the Coarse and
Refined method still outperformed the joint scheme.
Fourthly, we evaluate  the effectiveness of our proposed
modified Rayleigh Distance. This criterion is based on the
users’ beam width. We select FUs near the boundaries of the
modified Rayleigh Distance at different angles θ (see Fig. 4a),
and then compare  the average achievable rate of  far-field
beamforming  for  500  single-user  cases.  Specifically, we
analyze whether users classified as FUs under our modified
Rayleigh Distance achieve a high achievable rate when using
only the far-field codeword.

The reference SNR is set at 20dB
to minimize noise effects. The results are shown in Fig. 12.
The  far-field DFT beamforming vector a(θˆ) outperforms
the near-field codeword b(θˆ, rˆ) obtained via the exhaustive
search, which are  respectively  labeled as  ”Far-field” and.

In this paper, we analyze the received beam patterns of
NUs using a DFT codebook and propose a cost-effective beam
training method leveraging the properties of the beam pattern.
We derive a closed-form expression for the beam pattern width
and central beam pattern gain, which is further utilized to

9
establish a new distance threshold for distinguishing between
near-field and far-field users and facilitating near-field beam
training. The proposed beam training scheme consists of a
single stage of beam sweeping with the DFT codebook.  It
features  low overhead and complexity while demonstrating
excellent performance, particularly in high-SNR scenarios.
